customCast.name: Donald Trump
customCast.description: Donald Trump has a highly recognizable public appearance built around a formal, high-visibility political image. He is usually seen in tailored dark business suits with broad shoulders and structured lines that emphasize a traditional executive style. His ties are often long and vivid, commonly in strong colors like red or blue, creating a visual contrast against white dress shirts. His posture tends to project confidence and stage presence, often standing with squared shoulders and deliberate hand gestures that have become closely associated with his public persona. His facial expression can shift quickly from a measured, serious look to animated reactions during speeches or debates. His hairstyle has remained one of his most distinctive visual traits for decades, styled carefully and instantly recognizable from a distance. In dramatic scenes, cinematic lighting would likely emphasize sharp contrasts across his face, creating the image of a leader standing under pressure while surrounded by maps, advisors, and glowing screens. In a geopolitical thriller setting, he would visually fit the role of a commanding figure seated at the center of a tense command room, with the weight of unfolding events reflected in his expressions.

customCast.name: Benjamin Netanyahu
customCast.description: Benjamin Netanyahu presents the image of a long-serving statesman with a formal and disciplined appearance. He is typically seen in dark suits with clean tailoring and understated ties that reinforce a polished political presence. His overall style leans toward restrained and traditional rather than flashy, emphasizing authority through simplicity. He carries himself with an upright posture and often projects a composed, calculating expression. His facial features and mannerisms can create an impression of concentration, especially in settings involving security discussions or televised addresses. During public appearances, his expressions often appear measured, with an emphasis on seriousness and control. In a cinematic adaptation, lighting would likely highlight his focused gaze and the tension of decision-making under pressure. He visually fits scenes involving war rooms, televised statements, and strategic discussions. A thriller version of his appearance might place him standing before digital maps, illuminated by screens displaying military movements and crisis updates. His visual identity works naturally in political drama because it combines formality with the sense of a leader carrying responsibility during moments of national uncertainty and rapidly changing events.

customCast.name: Mojtaba Khamenei
customCast.description:
Mojtaba Khamenei presents a restrained and controlled public image that matches his reputation as a figure who operated for years largely outside the spotlight before rising into visible leadership. He is typically seen in traditional clerical attire associated with senior figures in Iran's religious establishment. His appearance is generally formal and understated rather than theatrical, emphasizing authority through symbolism and composure rather than dramatic visual presentation. Public photographs often show him wearing a dark robe over lighter clerical garments, accompanied by a black or dark-colored turban associated with descendants of the Prophet Muhammad in Shi'a tradition.

His face often carries a serious and thoughtful expression, with controlled body language and limited outward emotion. Rather than projecting overt charisma or forceful public energy, his visual presence is quieter and more reserved. Analysts and reports have frequently described him as someone who spent much of his influence operating behind the scenes within networks around Iran's religious and security institutions.

For a geopolitical thriller adaptation, cinematic framing could emphasize low lighting, long corridors, guarded rooms, and quiet moments of decision rather than dramatic speeches. He would appear seated at large wooden tables surrounded by intelligence reports and maps, while aides move in and out of the room in tense silence. The visual effect is less that of a public campaign figure and more of a person presented as operating within tightly controlled systems of power.

customCast.name: Bob Brikko
customCast.description: Bob Brikko looks like someone who has spent decades walking into situations everybody else was running away from. He is in his late fifties or early sixties, with weathered facial features that suggest years of travel, long nights, and constant exposure to chaotic environments. His hair is thick but slightly untamed, with streaks of gray that seem earned rather than styled. He wears a practical field jacket layered over shirts with rolled sleeves and often carries a microphone in one hand while papers and notes are stuffed into pockets. There is always a little dust on his boots and the feeling that he arrived directly from another crisis zone without stopping. His eyes are sharp and constantly scanning his surroundings, as if searching for the next important detail before anyone else notices it. He has a rugged reporter's face that shifts naturally between seriousness and dry humor. In a thriller scene, Bob would stand beneath flashing emergency lights while wind blows through his jacket and distant sirens echo behind him. He looks exactly like someone who reports history while standing in the middle of it.

customCast.name: Israeli Pilot
customCast.description: The Israeli pilot has the appearance of someone shaped by discipline, routine, and high-pressure responsibility. Around his early forties, he has an athletic build that reflects years of military conditioning rather than bodybuilder aesthetics. His face is defined and practical, with little concern for unnecessary style. Short dark hair sits neatly beneath a flight helmet, and his expression often remains controlled even during tense moments. His eyes carry the focused look of someone trained to process information quickly and make decisions under pressure. He wears a flight suit covered with mission patches and technical equipment, with straps, gloves, and survival gear attached with functional precision. Small signs of fatigue around his eyes hint at sleepless nights and repeated deployments. In cinematic moments, he would often appear illuminated by instrument panels inside a cockpit, with reflections of warning lights moving across his face. During scenes on an airfield, wind would move across his uniform while aircraft engines thunder in the background. He visually represents a professional caught between duty, personal cost, and the consequences that emerge after difficult decisions.

movies.plot:
- Pre-dawn over the Strait of Hormuz. The horizon is doing that thing it does in June — bleeding gold into black in a way that could pass for beautiful if it weren't for the Apache helicopter sitting thirty feet below the surface, and the oil slick spreading outward in a slow, patient circle behind Bob Brikko's borrowed rubber Zodiac. Bob stands in the inflatable with the composure of a man who surrendered the concept of dry boots sometime in 2019 but has not surrendered broadcast journalism. His sand-colored field jacket is damp from the collar down. He holds his microphone with one hand and a laminated talking-points sheet with the other — though the sheet hasn't been consulted since a far quieter spring. On the horizon, the autonomous sea drone that pulled two US Army soldiers from the wreck is still circling in slow arcs, as if unwilling to vacate the scene. The soldiers are safe. The helicopter is not. A seabird lands on the rim of the Zodiac, considers Bob, and decides this is also its broadcast. Bob looks at the camera. He looks at the bird. He looks back at the camera. He has been here before, in this Zodiac, on this water, in this light. The light is always the same. The reasons keep updating. --- Bob says: "Behind me, the sea is on fire — again!"

  - The Oval Office smells of cherry-vanilla diet soda and fresh decisions. Three television screens are tuned to three different networks; all three show the same aerial footage of the Strait of Hormuz, with chyrons that have evolved since dawn from INCIDENT to ESCALATION to something the networks' legal teams are still arguing about. Donald Trump stands at the Resolute Desk — not seated, because a seated president is a thinking president and a standing president is an announcing one. On the desk: an uncapped Sharpie, a hand-drawn map of Iran with several sites circled and one circled twice and one circled three times with a star alongside it, and a single page labeled STRIKE OPTIONS that someone has attempted to format like a menu. An aide enters with a fresh intelligence briefing. Trump does not read it. He has already read the television. The aide waits. The Sharpie moves. A fourth circle appears on the map, around a location that is possibly a city and possibly a smudge from a previous session. The aide photographs it discreetly, because that is what aides do now, at this particular juncture in history. The aide exits. The screens keep scrolling. The Sharpie is recapped. --- Trump says: "Iran knocked down our most beautiful, perfect helicopter — sad!"

  - The building is government-issue beige — that particular shade of administrative neutrality that communicates nothing to satellites. Mojtaba Khamenei sits at a long conference table with six advisors, two generals, one communications director who has been silently composing a statement for forty-five minutes, and a single window through which the Tehran skyline is visible — intact, lit, moving through its morning as though the Strait of Hormuz were very far away, which geopolitically it is not. A tablet is slid face-up across the table. Trump's statement is on it. The words MASSIVE RETALIATION are rendered in capital letters. Mojtaba reads it without touching the tablet. He reads it a second time. He turns the tablet face-down with a precision that is not quite calm and not quite its opposite. The communications director looks up from his draft. Mojtaba straightens the pen in front of him until it is perfectly parallel to the table's edge. The generals watch this small adjustment. The advisors watch it too. The communications director adds two sentences to the draft and deletes four, then stares at what remains. Outside, Tehran traffic moves in its ordinary rhythms. The oil price is climbing past ninety-eight dollars a barrel. The window catches the morning light and refuses to let go. --- Mojtaba says: "Our air defense was conducting a routine maintenance calibration!"

  - Altitude: classified. Position: above the southern Lebanese coast. Below, the city of Tyre — Phoenician, ancient, Mediterranean, currently in the targeting display's field of resolution — appears as a cool green topographic abstraction, coordinates resolving into clean geometric shapes that bear no visual resemblance to the buildings they represent. The Pilot's gloved hands rest on the controls with practiced neutrality. The canopy reflects instrument light in small scatter patterns across the visor. An hour ago the radio carried Tehran's warning — that Iran could resume hostilities if Israeli strikes on Hezbollah continued — and since then the radio has been quiet, as if both sides agreed that the message was received and the outcome was already determined. The evacuation orders for Tyre were issued. Some residents complied and left. Some did not. The targeting display does not indicate which. Iran's warning has been logged and filed. The file of warnings that precede actions is extensive. It takes up, at this point, a considerable amount of server space. The Mediterranean, from this altitude, is a seamless dark mirror. Nothing about forty thousand feet feels like consequence. That gap — between the instrument panel and the street — is both an engineering achievement and the central problem of the evening. --- Pilot says: "They warned us to stop, so naturally we continued!"

  - The situation room is lit by the kind of overhead fluorescent that makes everyone present look like they haven't slept, because they haven't. Netanyahu stands before the illuminated regional map — the one that used to show the Middle East in broad categorical colors, which is now blanketed in a constellation of updated markers: red for active strikes, amber for active threats, yellow for incidents, and a new purple category the technician added two days ago and has not yet formally named. The BBC analysis is printed and on the table: TRUMP AND NETANYAHU RISK A PERMACRISIS. Netanyahu has read it. He has read it three times, which is two more than he reads things he agrees with. The word "permacrisis" is doing something to the room's atmosphere. It is the kind of compound construction that arrives in the third year of something and retroactively explains why the first two years felt like forward motion. An aide gestures at the Hormuz cluster. The amber markers from last night have turned red. The Lebanese indicators have multiplied. The West Bank now has its own weather system, separate from the others. Netanyahu picks up a pointer, sets it down, picks it up again. The map does not improve with pointing. He points anyway. --- Netanyahu says: "I meant reshape the region, not the insurance maps!"

  - Tyre, Lebanon. Post-strike. The street behind Bob Brikko is a sedimentary record of the past several hours: concrete dust still hanging at chest height, the horizontal ruin of a wall that is no longer a wall, the scattered geometry of a kitchen — one tiled surface, a pot, the abstract remains of something that was a refrigerator. The air has that gray-white particulate quality that tastes of construction and its opposite. In the background, a slow convoy of vehicles moves north along the coast road. Families who received the Israeli military's evacuation notice and complied. One man is transporting an armoire lashed to the roof of a Fiat. Another carries two children and a birdcage. The bird, it must be said, appears entirely unaffected. It is singing something. Bob has filed dispatches from Tyre before. He was here in 2006. He was here in 2024. He is here now, holding the same model of microphone, a new battery pack, and an expression that reached its terminal form years ago and has maintained it since. Nine dead. The evacuation order was issued in advance. The Iranian warning was also issued in advance. The strike proceeded. Bob watches the caged bird singing on the back of a moving truck. The bird does not seem to subscribe to any available news service. --- Bob says: "Nine killed in Tyre — the warnings were very clear!"

  - White House Situation Room. The banks of screens cycle between satellite feeds of the Strait of Hormuz, a site in northeastern Iran, and a vessel in the Arabian Gulf. Military advisors are arranged in tiered seating around the central table. Trump stands at the center. He has not sat down in forty-five minutes. Sitting is contemplative; standing is decisional. The operation is underway. The briefing characterized it as "precision." The satellite feeds are confirming "precision" in real time, which from orbital altitude resembles a small bright point, a pause, and then a different quality of nothing. A general raises the question of second-phase authorization. Trump nods — the specific nod that advisors have learned to document immediately, because it is the nod of a decision already made. The general notes this. He writes something on the briefing pad in front of him. A communications aide waits near the door holding draft statement version twelve. Versions one through eleven have all been shortened. The current text is nine words long. Trump will expand it. On one screen's lower corner, the oil price ticker reads one hundred and two dollars. It is moving with the quiet inevitability of a thing that has been moving in this direction for some time and sees no reason to stop. --- Trump says: "The strike was perfect, tremendous, like nothing you've seen!"

  - Underground. The room is low-ceilinged and purpose-built in the way rooms designed for contingencies always are — everything bolted or bracketed, nothing decorative, a floor-to-ceiling map covering the entire east wall, a coffee maker that has been running since yesterday evening. The fluorescent fixtures hum at a frequency that is slightly wrong. Mojtaba does not appear to notice, or has decided not to. On the table: a report on the US strike and its confirmed sites. A second report on civilian conditions across Tehran — markets volatile, supply chains strained, the word "uncertainty" appearing in every paragraph. A third document summarizing Al Jazeera's street coverage: Iranians navigating daily life under conditions that one interviewee described, simply, as "anything but normal." The phrase has been highlighted. It does not need to be. The oil-price feed occupies its own monitor, visible from Mojtaba's chair. One hundred and two. One hundred and three. The movement is too slow for optimism and too consistent to dismiss. An aide brings tea. The tea is genuinely good. No one remarks on this. The US strike has hit three sites. All three are listed as military infrastructure in the official American statement, and as something more nuanced in the unofficial one. Mojtaba studies the east wall. Iran is large. The map confirms this. --- Mojtaba says: "A hundred dollars a barrel — this is fine, technically!"

  - The briefing memo is three pages. Its implications run considerably longer. Netanyahu reads it at a desk inside the Kirya — Jerusalem's defense compound — beneath a framed photograph of himself with a former US president, which has migrated from the wall's center to its left side over the past several months in a series of small, unremarked adjustments. The memo: the United Kingdom, France, Germany, Canada, Australia, and one further country have imposed sanctions on Israeli settler-violence networks operating in the occupied West Bank. France, acting separately, has formally barred Bezalel Smotrich from entry to French territory. Smotrich had no imminent travel to France scheduled. He was not planning to go to France. The barring is nonetheless formal, issued in French, with the precise bureaucratic language the French deploy for things they find both offensive and administratively manageable. Netanyahu turns to the page listing the sanctioned entities. Several names are familiar. Several of the networks have addresses in areas the memo refers to as "disputed." Netanyahu underlines the word with a pencil — which is how he annotates things he considers factually contested — and continues reading. His phone buzzes. Trump. Netanyahu looks at the phone. Then at the memo. Then back at the phone. He answers. --- Netanyahu says: "They sanctioned the settlers but not the settlement — noted!"

  - Bob Brikko is back on the Zodiac. The sun has traced its full arc. It is setting now rather than rising — a full day elapsed between this scene and the cold open — and the sea behind him has graduated from oil-slick shimmer to that saturated crimson the Mediterranean produces when it wants to editorialize. The sea drone is gone. The Apache wreckage is still down there. The strike on Iran has happened. Nine people are dead in Tyre. The evacuation convoy is still moving north along the Lebanese coast road, the armoire still lashed to the Fiat's roof, now silhouetted in orange — traveling somewhere, at convoy speed, with its contents intact and its destination unclear. Oil is at one hundred and four dollars. Bob holds his microphone at his side. His laminated talking-points sheet has been folded into a small square and tucked into his breast pocket — not because he needed it today, but because that is the pocket for things that might be needed on the way back. He looks at the camera with the expression of a man who has filed dispatches from worse days than this, and who knows — the way field reporters accumulate knowledge, which is mostly just exposure — that the bar for "worse" has recently been recalibrated. The seabird from this morning is back on the Zodiac's rim. Different bird, probably. Same posture. The sea does not comment. Bob says: "We'll be back tomorrow — if there still is one!"

scenes.title: Dawn's Reflection
scenes.story: The pre-dawn scene unfolds over the Strait of Hormuz, with the horizon blending gold into black. Bob Brikko, in his damp sand-colored field jacket, stands on the Zodiac holding a microphone and a neglected talking-points sheet. An autonomous sea drone circles slowly, while a seabird lands on the Zodiac's rim, observing. The atmosphere is tense, highlighting the contrast between the serene light and the underlying turmoil.
scenes.dialogue: Behind me, the sea is on fire — again!

scenes.title: Tension in the Oval Office
scenes.story: The Oval Office is bathed in the glow of television screens displaying escalating chyrons. Donald Trump stands by the Resolute Desk, surrounded by strategic markers. An aide brings an intelligence briefing, Trump's focus remains on the screens.
scenes.dialogue: Iran knocked down our most beautiful, perfect helicopter — sad!

scenes.title: High-Stakes Decision
scenes.story: Mojtaba Khamenei sits at a long conference table surrounded by advisors and generals. A tablet with Trump's 'MASSIVE RETALIATION' statement is slid across. Mojtaba reads it twice, then adjusts a pen with precise tension. Outside, Tehran remains oblivious to the escalating tensions.
scenes.dialogue: Our air defense was conducting a routine maintenance calibration!

scenes.title: Above Tyre - Crisis in the Air
scenes.story: The Israeli pilot, hands steady on controls, gazes at the resolution of Tyre below. The targeting display shows green geometric shapes, distant yet ominous. The radio's silence hangs heavy, echoing Tehran's warning. The Mediterranean mirrors the sky's darkness, a vast expanse devoid of consequence.
scenes.dialogue: They warned us to stop, so naturally we continued!

scenes.title: Room of Decisions
scenes.story: The situation room buzzes under harsh fluorescent lights, casting shadows on weary faces. Netanyahu stands by the updated regional map, colors shifting from amber to red. The weight of 'permacrisis' lingers in the air, amplifying tension. An aide gestures at the escalating markers. Netanyahu's futile pointing reflects the struggle for control.
scenes.dialogue: I meant reshape the region, not the insurance maps!

scenes.title: Aftermath in Tyre
scenes.story: The street in Tyre is a scene of devastation post-strike, with concrete dust hanging in the air and ruins scattered around. A slow convoy of evacuating families moves north along the coast road. One man transports an armoire on a Fiat, another carries children and a birdcage. The bird on the truck sings unaffected. Bob Brikko, a seasoned reporter, observes the aftermath, reflecting on the clear warnings preceding the tragedy.
scenes.dialogue: Nine killed in Tyre — the warnings were very clear!

scenes.title: Decision at the Heart of Power
scenes.story: The White House Situation Room hums with tension. Military advisors watch screens cycling through critical feeds. Trump stands unwavering, embodying decisive leadership. Precision operation unfolds visually. General seeks second-phase approval. Trump nods confidently, shaping the future. Oil prices climb, echoing inevitable escalation.
scenes.dialogue: The strike was perfect, tremendous, like nothing you've seen!

scenes.title: Underground Contemplation
scenes.story: Mojtaba silently contemplates amidst the dimly lit underground room filled with maps and reports. The fluorescent hum adds an eerie undertone. Reports on US strike sites and Tehran's civilian conditions lay on the table. The oil-price feed ticks unforgivingly. An aide brings tea, unnoticed. Mojtaba gazes at the vast map, encapsulating Iran's expanse.
scenes.dialogue: A hundred dollars a barrel — this is fine, technically!

scenes.title: Critical Annotations
scenes.story: Netanyahu reads the memo under the watchful eye of his own image, surrounded by geopolitical implications. The memo lists sanctions on settler-violence networks in the West Bank. He underlines 'disputed' with a pencil, signaling his scrutiny of the factual context. A phone call from Trump adds to the charged atmosphere.
scenes.dialogue: They sanctioned the settlers but not the settlement — noted!

scenes.title: Twilight Reflections
scenes.story: Bob Brikko stands on the Zodiac, the sun setting, sea now crimson. The aftermath visible: wreckage, convoy, oil price surge. Bob holds his microphone, reflecting on past crises. A seabird perched, silent witness.
scenes.dialogue: We'll be back tomorrow — if there still is one!
